metadata.title: Exploring examinees' responses to constructed response items with a supervised topic model
metadata.journal: British Journal of Mathematical and Statistical Psychology

abstract: Textual data are increasingly common in test data as many assessments include constructed response (CR) items as indicators of participants' understanding. The development of techniques based on natural language processing has made it possible for researchers to rapidly analyse large sets of textual data. One family of statistical techniques for this purpose are probabilistic topic models. Topic modelling is a technique for detecting the latent topic structure in a collection of documents and has been widely used to analyse texts in a variety of areas. The detected topics can reveal primary themes in the documents, and the relative use of topics can be useful in investigating the variability of the documents. Supervised latent Dirichlet allocation (SLDA) is a popular topic model in that family that jointly models textual data and paired responses such as could occur with participants' textual answers to CR items and their rubric-based scores. SLDA has an assumption of a homogeneous relationship between textual data and paired responses across all documents. This approach, while useful for some purposes, may not be satisfied for situations in which a population has subgroups that have different relationships. In this study, we introduce a new supervised topic model that incorporates finite-mixture modelling into the SLDA. This new model can detect latent groups of participants that have different relationships between their textual responses and associated scores. The model is illustrated with an example from an analysis of a set of textual responses and paired scores from a middle grades assessment of science inquiry knowledge. A simulation study is presented to investigate the performance of the proposed model under practical testing conditions.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Exploring examinees' responses to constructed response items with a supervised topic model
paragraphs: Textual data have become increasingly common in test data as many assessments include constructed response (CR) items as indicators of examinees' understanding (Attali, 2014). CR items ask examinees to produce their answers rather than to select options from a given list (Livingston, 2009;Yang et al., 2018). CR items do not typically have just a single 'right' answer. Rather, there are usually multiple ways of answering correctly. As a result, these items are often more flexible than selected response items for measuring higher-order thinking and reasoning skills (Attali, 2014;Livingston, 2009;Tankersley, 2007).

Examinees' responses to CR items have been shown to provide rich information about their thinking and reasoning. Buxton et al. (2013), for example, found instructionally useful information from a qualitative analysis of the text of students' responses to CR items in a middle grades test of science inquiry knowledge. Buxton et al. showed how teachers could use this information to inform instruction about their students' comprehension of science inquiry concepts. Dutro et al. (2013) qualitatively analysed writings of four students on state and district assessments, focusing on features such as structure, cohesion, focus, word choice and sentence fluency. The authors indicated that the rubric-based scores alone were not sufficient to capture students' strengths and weaknesses in writing.

The development of technologies based on natural language processing (NLP) has made it possible for researchers to rapidly analyse large sets of textual data. NLP consists of computer algorithms for analysing natural language, including, but not limited to, counting word frequencies, sentiment analysis, automatic translation and speech recognition (Chowdhury, 2003;Jurafsky & Martin, 2008;Manning & Schütze, 1999). Blei et al. (2003), in particular, developed a type of statistical modelling, using NLP and machine learning, called probabilistic topic modelling (in this paper, we refer to this simply as topic modelling) for detecting the latent thematic structure in very large corpora of text, such as detecting the dominate themes in abstracts of scientific journals over a period of decades. In topic modelling, a document is the unit of analysis and is typically viewed as an unordered set of words. Topics are described using probability mass functions over a fixed set of words such that, for a given topic, each word has a probability of appearing in a document in the corpus. In addition, each document represents a mixture of topics with distinct proportions.

Examinees' responses to a CR item can consist of a variety of different words to respond to the same prompt. In analysing examinees' written responses, topic models have been used to detect the latent topics or themes in the response data that represent the textual data as well as information about the relative use of the topics in each response. The topics can be useful in understanding primary themes in written responses, and the relative use of topics can be useful in investigating the variability in examinees' written responses. Cardozo-Gaibisso et al. (2020) and Kim et al. (2017), for example, used a topic model for smaller samples to detect evidence of examinees' thinking and reasoning aside from that which may be captured by rubricbased scores. Choi et al. (2021) used mastery status from a diagnostic classification model (Rupp et al., 2010) as covariates in a topic model to investigate how examinees' mastery status of three different reading skills influenced their writing patterns. Results showed that mastery of the skills of integration of knowledge and ideas was significantly related to examinees' use of a topic reflecting successful integrative borrowing.

Text data are often paired with additional information, such as essay scores or articles with authors' names. Topic modelling can be used to describe the relationship between examinees' written responses and related scores. One of the simplest and frequently used topic models is latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA; Blei et al., 2003). This model is considered as an unsupervised model in that it only uses the information in the textual data to detect latent topics. LDA has been extended in various ways since it was introduced. Blei and McAuliffe (2008) developed the supervised LDA (SLDA) in which an external response (e.g., test scores) can be used to supervise the detection of latent topics in a text corpus. SLDA has been used to detect relationships between topics and associated scores. Xiong et al. (2020), for example, used SLDA to investigate the relationship between topics detected from examinees' responses on a social studies test and associated rubric-based scores. Copur-Gencturk et al. (2022) used SLDA to detect the relationship between topics and scores of middle grades mathematics teachers on a test of proportional reasoning. The results of that study suggested that the topics extracted from the text of teachers' responses reflected differences detected in a qualitative analysis of their reasoning on the same concepts. Further, use of some of the topics in the model clearly reflected understanding of proportional reasoning, while use of other topics in the model reflected a lack of understanding of the construct.

Applying SLDA in assessment data assumes that the examinees share a homogeneous relationship between topics and associated test scores. However, examinees' writing performance may vary substantially even though they are responding to the same prompt. For example, while some examinees may borrow sentences from a given passage and integrate them into their answers, other examinees may simply copy sentences from the passage without demonstrating any attempt to integrate (Choi et al., 2021). SLDA by itself, however, is not always able to detect different relationships between topics and scores, for example, when examinees use different response strategies. Thus, results from the SLDA model alone might be misleading by inferences from the model that are not representative of the study sample.

In this study, we propose a new approach that incorporates finite-mixture modelling into the SLDA to detect latent groups of examinees in the sample that have different relationships between their responses to CR items and the scores of their responses. In the proposed model, examinees' scores are represented by a finite set of equations, each of which corresponds to a unique relationship between topic proportions and scores. As with other topic models, the proposed model also captures latent topics from written responses and estimates the relative use of topics for each response. Identifying latent subgroups that share the same relationship between written responses and associated scores can add to the information about examinees' performance on the assessment.

For model estimation and inferences, we used Bayesian methods in this study. Bayesian methods have been shown to be useful in various fields as they have many advantages, including, but not limited to, (i) the ability to incorporating prior knowledge into estimation, (ii) the ability to conducting inferences for complex models using advanced statistical algorithms, and (iii) evaluating model performance using modern graphic tools.

We first provide a brief overview of LDA and SLDA, and then we introduce the proposed model, finite-mixture SLDA (MixSLDA). Next, an analysis of empirical data consisting of examinees' responses to CR items and associated scores is presented to illustrate the application of MixSLDA. A simulation study is then presented to evaluate the performance of MixSLDA under various practical testing conditions. Conclusions and discussion are provided in the final section.

sections.header_raw: EXPLORING CONSTRUCTED RESPONSE ITEMS WITH A TOPIC MODEL
sections.header_norm: exploring constructed response items with a topic model
paragraphs: In LDA, each document is assumed to be composed of a mixture of topics, and each word (w i,n ) in a document has a topic indicator (z i,n ) indicating the topic from which this word came. The topic assignment is determined by a K × 1 topic proportion vector i = i1 , i2 , … , iK T for document i. LDA assumes that each word in a document is randomly drawn from a corresponding topic. In other words, the order of words in the document does not matter. This is referred to as the bag-of-words assumption (Blei, 2012). Topic proportions ( i ) and word probabilities ( k ) for each topic are assumed to follow a Dirichlet distribution.

sections.header_norm: finite mixture supervised latent dirichlet allocation
paragraphs: In this study, we focus on continuous types of responses associated with documents. paragraphs: In this section, we illustrate the use of the MixSLDA with data from an NSF-funded project designed to train teachers to teach science inquiry skills to middle grade and high school students. In this example, we address the following questions using a data set obtained from a science inquiry test that was administered as a part of the project: (i) What are the latent themes underlying students' answers? (ii) Are there any subgroups that have different relationships between topic proportions and scores? (iii) What are the relationships between topic proportions and scores?

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Exploring examinees' responses to constructed response items with a supervised topic model
paragraphs: The test was designed to measure middle grades students' use of academic language and understanding of science inquiry practices. The test specifically focused on the understanding of cause and effect, independent and dependent variables, and construction of hypotheses. The test consisted of seven CR testlets, each with from two to five items. Each item was scored from 2 to 4 points. The total of the item scores over all seven testlets was used as the response variable for the MixSLDA.

Tests were administered once at the beginning of the year as a pre-test and a second time at the end of the year as a post-test. To counter memory effects between pre-test and post-test, two different forms of the test were constructed, Form A and Form B. Both forms had parallel testlet structures. One of the two forms was randomly given to students for the pre-test, and the other form was given for the post-test. For purposes of this example, pre-test and post-test responses from only Form A were used in this analysis. As the main purpose of this example was to illustrate the proposed model using empirical data, the focus was on understanding examinees' writing performances rather than performance on individual items. Therefore, each student's answers to the CR items from all seven testlets were combined and considered as one answer document to detect latent themes from the whole response and capture the association between students' written responses and their scores. Thus, in this analysis, each student had one answer document with an associated score. Note that students in the pre-test data and students in the post-test data were different as students alternated forms between the pre-and post-tests.

paragraphs: The sample for this example consisted of 533 answer documents: 277 documents (51.97%) were from the pre-test, and 256 documents (48.03%) were from the post-test. There were 280 documents (52.53%) from seventh grade students, 252 documents (47.28%) from eighth grade students, and one document (.19%) from a sixth grade student. Additionally, 246 documents (46.15%) and 287 documents (53.85%) were from female and male students, respectively. The mean score for the 533 answers was 9.66 with a standard deviation of 5.51.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Exploring examinees' responses to constructed response items with a supervised topic model
paragraphs: Before analysing data using topic models, we took a series of pre-processing steps in order for the topic models to provide more meaningful and interpretable results. There are different types of steps for preprocessing (e.g., Boyd-Graber et al., 2014) designed to be useful for helping ensure the interpretability of the resulting topic model structure. The pre-processing steps include removing punctuations, tokenization, normalization and removing stopwords.

In pre-processing of the data, a student's response to the CR item was considered as a single document, and the whole response was broken into words based on spaces between tokens. This procedure is called text tokenization and treats a document as an unordered list of words. Although different orders of words can deliver different messages, this bag-of-words approach is considered sufficient to convey the general latent ideas of a corpus of text (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013).

In the normalization step, different forms of a word were converted to the same root meaning. This improves the probability of words clustering together, thereby improving interpretability of the resulting topic structure. In this study, normalization included the following: plural words were converted to singular words, past tense verbs were converted to present tense verbs, capitalized words were converted to lowercase, and typographical errors were replaced with the correct spelling. One issue that tends to occur with student-generated data on examinations is that they often have various kinds of typographical errors. Correcting typographical errors improves the focus on detecting the latent themes in the responses rather than focusing on grammatical correctness.

Stopwords are high frequency words that do not convey significant meaning. This is a contextdependent decision but typically includes words such as 'a', 'the', 'and', 'is'. Since stopwords tend to appear frequently, they can potentially dominate the topic structure, making it difficult to interpret the topics in the model. We found that stopwords needed to be chosen carefully. There are some R packages that provide stopword lists (e.g., Feinerer & Hornik, 2018;Grün & Hornik, 2018;Roberts et al., 2020). These can be helpful, but stopwords are also context-dependent in that some words can be classified as stopwords for some sets of documents, but not for other sets. In this study, we manually selected stopwords rather than using a list of words reported by others. This was possible with these data because the vocabulary of examinees' responses tended to be constrained by the passages and the requirements of the questions, so the vocabulary size was manageable.

For this study, we only considered words that had a frequency of occurrence of at least 10 across all documents, because those words with frequencies of less than 10 tended to be used by a single examinee and did not contribute much to formation of topics. This removal of words reduced the vocabulary size, but this reduced representation of text has been shown to be sufficient for extracting meaningful latent thematic information from texts (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013;Hopkins & King, 2010). The final data set for the example consisted of answers from 533 examinees containing 421 unique words with an average document length after pre-processing of 86.57 words (SD = 37.87 words).

paragraphs: As shown in the next section, a series of MixSLDA models were fitted to the assessment data to determine the number of topics and the number of latent classes in the best-fitting model. The following priors were used:

Here, j V refers to a V -dimensional vector that has all elements as 1. In those cases where more than one latent class was presented, we put constraints on the regression coefficients to avoid label switching among latent classes (Gelman et al., 2013;McLachlan & Peel, 2000). For example, in the MixSLDA analysis described in the results section, we restricted the coefficient for Topic 3 in Class 2 to be larger than that for Class 1 to make the two latent classes distinguishable. In total, 40,000 iterations were run, with the first 15,000 iterations being discarded as burn-in, and the next 25,000 iterations were used for estimating the posterior. Convergence was checked by examining trace plots and Geweke's diagnostic test (Geweke, 1992), which is implemented in the R package coda (Plummer et al., 2006).

In those cases where more than one latent class was presented, we put constraints on the regression coefficients to avoid label switching among latent classes (Gelman et al., 2013;McLachlan & Peel, 2000). For example, in the MixSLDA analysis described in the results section, we restricted the coefficient for Topic 3 in Class 2 to be larger than that for Class 1 to make the two latent classes distinguishable. In total, 40,000 iterations were run, with the first 15,000 iterations being discarded as burn-in, and the next 25,000 iterations were used for estimating the posterior. Convergence was checked by examining trace plots and Geweke's diagnostic test (Geweke, 1992), which is implemented in the R package coda (Plummer et al., 2006).

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2024 - Exploring examinees' responses to constructed response items with a supervised topic model
sections.header_raw: | Determining the number of topics and latent classes in the model
sections.header_norm: determining the number of topics and latent classes in the model
paragraphs: In this example, we used an exploratory approach to determine the best-fitting model. In this approach, models with different numbers of latent classes and topics were explored and compared. The number of latent classes and number of topics need to be pre-specified to fit a given model. In the mixture modelling literature, the number of latent classes has been often determined by model comparison information indices (e.g., Kim et al., 2021;Li et al., 2009;Lubke & Neale, 2006). In the case of determining the number of topics, several methods have been used to help in determining the number of topics such as calculating perplexity (e.g., Blei et al., 2003), topic coherence (Mimno et al., 2011) and exclusivity (Bischof & Airoldi, 2012;Roberts et al., 2019). In this example, we considered two information indices, the widely applicable information criterion (WAIC; Watanabe, 2010) and the leave-one-out crossvalidation (LOOCV; Gelman et al., 2013;Vehtari et al., 2017), to determine the number of topics and number of latent classes. Both indices indicate the best-fitting models as having the smallest values. In this example, we fit topic models with from 2 to 7 topics and 1 to 3 latent classes. Table 1 shows WAIC and LOOCV values for each of the solutions. WAIC and LOOCV both suggested the best-fitting model was one with 4 topics and 2 latent classes.

We further examined topic coherence and exclusivity, assuming that two latent classes were optimal. Topic coherence and exclusivity have been shown to be correlated well with human judgement of topic quality (Mimno et al., 2011). The topic coherence measure has high values when high probability words co-occur in the same document. The exclusivity measure has high values when high probability words in one topic do not appear as high probability words for other topics. Figure 2 displays topic coherence and exclusivity of the six candidate models. It shows that the four-topic solution has reasonable topic coherence and exclusivity.

paragraphs: In this section, we present results from the MixSLDA model with four topics and two latent classes, as well as results from the one latent class solution (i.e., the non-mixture SLDA solution) for the purpose of comparison. Table 2 presents the 20 words that have the highest probabilities for each of the four topics in the MixSLDA model. Topics from the SLDA model were similar to those in Table 2, and Figure 3 provides evidence that topics obtained from MixSLDA and SLDA analyses were similar. In Figure 3, the horizontal axis shows the top 10 highest probability words for each of the topics from the MixSLDA (the upper panel) and the SLDA (the lower panel). A darker tile indicates that a corresponding word has a higher probability for the topic. Topics from both models appeared to have very similar patterns of colours.

Each of the topics was examined along with documents that had the highest use of a corresponding topic. Words in the Topic 1 row were mostly about questions for cause and effect. Words in the Topic 2 row were generally about questions for hypothesis, observation and evidence. Words in the Topic 3 row mostly included answers about controlling of variables. Words in the Topic 4 row were mostly related to the use of everyday language. Most of the responses with high Topic 4 proportions did not include academic or scientific words.

paragraphs: Table 3 presents regression coefficient estimates for individual topics for predicting scores for Classes 1 (b 1 ) and 2 (b 2 ) from MixSLDA and regression estimates from SLDA (b). Mixing proportion estimates were .7 and .3 for Class 1 and Class 2, respectively. The coefficient estimates represent expected scores when an examinee uses words exclusively from a given topic. The general pattern of coefficients for Topics 1, 2, and 4 for this model was similar for both classes: Topics 1 and 4 had low coefficients, and Topic 2 had high coefficients. The coefficient estimates for Topic 3, however, were quite different for the two classes. Class 2 had a higher coefficient than Class 1, which suggests that examinees in the two classes have different expected scores even though they used words from the same topic.

Figure 4 presents the box plots of topic proportions for examinees' answer documents. It indicates that answer documents of examinees in Class 2 tend to have higher proportions on Topics 2 and 3, and lower proportions on Topic 4. Examinees in Class 2 had a higher average score (mean = 15.63; SD = 3.71) than examinees in Class 1 (mean = 8.85; SD = 5.20). Further, Class 2 appeared to have more male examinees (52% vs. 66%) and eighth grade examinees (46% vs. 59%).

paragraphs: T A B L E 2 Twenty words with the highest probabilities for each topic obtained from MixSLDA.
Topic 1: Cause and Effect (CE) fish, algae, small, effect, cause, population, increase, eat, because, converter, energy, catalytic, disease, decrease, large, kill, work, release, stone, most

Topic 2: Hypothesis, Observation, and Evidence (HOE) temperature, salt, same, increase, water, energy, algae, boil, because, faster, sheep, if, more, pour, viscosity, time, hotter, hypothesis, decease, honey Topic 3: Controlling Variables (CV) clone, sheep, weight, sick, fish, because, people, non, if, time, type, more, see, lifting, energy, often, honey, small, lift, catalytic Topic 4: Everyday Language (EVL) fish, because, more, eat, make, die, if, water, one, big, salt, all, honey, out, sick, hot, what, small, food, work

Additionally, we evaluated the model performance using posterior predictive checking (PPC; Gelman et al., 2013;Rubin, 1984). PPC compares data generated from the model and observed data. If a model is a good fit, then data generated from the model should be similar to the observed data. Figure 5 shows the distributions of responses from 3000 replicated data sets drawn from the posterior predictive distribution (thin grey curves) compared with the empirical distribution of the observed responses (thick dark curves). The MixSLDA better replicates the empirical distribution. An additional PPC study further indicated that the MixSLDA with two classes is complex enough to represent the observed data (see Appendix 2).

During estimation, label switching can occur because the likelihood is invariant with switched labels & Peel, 2000). In this study, we used weakly informative prior distributions for k and to deal with this issue. Specifically, the values for k were assigned to ensure the index of estimated topics corresponds to the index of simulated topics (see the prior for k ). In addition, we used the Hungarian algorithm (Hornik, 2005) when summarizing results to make the estimated topics match the simulated counterparts. We used = (3, 7) T (see the prior for ) to indicate the second latent class can have more subjects. The total number of iterations was 40,000, with the first 10,000 iterations used as burin-in. Our pilot study indicated that this burn-in period was sufficient for this simulation study. The subsequent 30,000 iterations were used for posterior inferences. Convergence was evaluated by Geweke's convergence test (Geweke, 1992), and only estimates from converged runs were used in subsequent analyses. More than 99% of the 200 replications were converged for all the conditions considered in this study.

The RB was obtained from the equation RB = 100 ̂ -, where ̂ is a posterior mean estimate of . This gives a percentage of bias relative to the true parameter value. Previous research (e.g., Curran et al., 1996;Finch et al., 1997;Flora & Curran, 2004;Forero et al., 2009) has suggested that RB values less than 10% could be considered as moderate bias, and RB values larger than 10% could be considered as substantial bias.

Table 4 presents mean RB, MSE and CovR for 2 and average values of RB, MSE and CovR across b 1 and b 2 components. In general, the mean RB and MSE decreased as the number of documents or average document length increased. The mean RB values for b 1 were all under 10%, and the mean RB values for b 2 were under or close to 10%, when the number of documents was over 500 and the document length was over 50. The 2 required almost 1000 documents to have a mean RB close to 10% regardless of the document length. Most of the CovRs for b 1 were larger than .90, and b 2 had CovR values close to .90. There was no consistent pattern for the CovR values for b 1 and b 2 , and the CovR for 2 appeared to increase as the number of documents increased from 300 to 1000.

Figure 7 plots the posterior mean estimates and standard errors of the mixing proportion components, 1 and 2 . The horizontal lines indicate corresponding data-generating values (i.e., .3 and .7). When the document length is given, increasing the number of documents helped reduce the standard error of estimate and estimation bias. Increasing document length also helped reduce the estimation bias and its standard error. When the number of documents was 1000, the mixing proportion estimates were very close to the data-generating value across all the document length conditions. When the number of documents was 500 or 1000, the mixing proportion estimates were nearly stable for document lengths of 50 or greater.

sections.header_norm: conc lusions a n d discus sion
sections.container_raw: T A B L E 1 WAIC and LOOCV values for the MixSLDA models.
sections.container_norm: t a b l e 1 waic and loocv values for the mixslda models
paragraphs: This study presented a new topic model for use in detecting the heterogeneity in relationships between examinees' responses to CR items and associated scores. In general, responses to CR items are often paired with scores, because the responses are usually graded by raters or by machine. Investigating the relationship between the responses and scores is informative, because it helps in understanding how examinees' scores are based on their written responses. In this study, we proposed a finite-mixture

SLDA model (MixSLDA) for analysing text data and associated scores from CR items. As with other topic models, topics identified from this model can be useful in understanding latent themes in written responses, and the relative use of topics can be useful in investigating the variability in examinees' written responses. This model was designed to detect meaningful heterogeneous latent subgroups with respect to the relationships between topic proportions in text and paired scores. In this model, scores were regressed on topic proportions, and the regression coefficients were allowed to differ across different latent classes. This model was illustrated by analysing an assessment data, and it showed that the MixSLDA could be a useful tool for gaining insights into what and how examinees write. The MixSLDA detected latent topics that summarized examinees' written responses and the relationships between the written responses and associated scores in a straightforward way. Four topics were detected in examinees' written responses, and two latent classes were detected in the relationships between examinees' responses and their scores. Examinees in Class 2 had a higher average score, with a larger proportion of male and eighth grade examinees than those assigned to Class 1. Answer documents of examinees in Class 2 showed to have higher proportions of Topics 2 (i.e., Hypothesis, Observation, and Evidence) and 3 (i.e., Controlling Variables), and lower proportions on Topic 4 (i.e., Everyday Language) compared to those of examinees in Class 1. Furthermore, the results showed that the relationship between scores and Topic 3 differed for the two latent classes. We compared documents with relatively higher Topic 3 proportions (i.e., documents with Topic 3 proportion estimates higher than .45) and focused on how examinees constructed answers for the Topic 3 related questions. Most of the documents from the examinees in Class 2 indicated appropriate responses regarding controlling variables. On the other hand, documents from Class 1 appeared to lack sufficient understanding about independent and dependent variables, as reflected in their use of wrong information in the passages.

We also demonstrated that MixSLDA provided more complete capture of the data than SLDA, which is identical to MixSLDA with one latent class. An SLDA analysis was conducted for the purpose of comparison. Topics from both SLDA and MixSLDA were quite similar, which was not surprising since the two models learned topics from the same text. However, our model comparison indices and posterior predictive checks indicated that SLDA was not sufficient to represent the observed data.

In this study, we observed that some words appeared among the top 20 highest probability words in more than one topic. This is because each word has a probability of occurring in a document for each topic, and some words had high probabilities for more than one topic. Although exclusivity is sometimes a desirable characteristic, mixed membership models such as the SLDA and MixSLDA both allow for this to occur. One way to have more unique topics is to increase the number of topics as indicated in Figure 2, thereby increasing exclusivity. However, in this case, this also can lead to decreasing topic coherence. Topic coherence is often higher for a smaller number of topics because the topics tend to be dominated by common words. On the other hand, as the number of topics increases, topics tend to become specific, resulting in higher exclusivity. The challenge lies in selecting a model that has high values on both topic coherence and exclusivity. In this study, when the number of topics was less than four, topics tended to be more abstract, and when the number of topics was greater than four, some topics tended to be more similar. This model was further investigated by conducting a simulation study under different conditions. Data were generated based on the empirical example data. Results suggested that parameter recovery was influenced by both document size and document length. That is, the mean relative bias and MSE tended to decrease as the number of documents or the average number of words in a document increased. It indicates that having either a large number of documents or documents with sufficient length of words is necessary to obtain accurate parameter estimates. Some assessments have CR items that do not require a long response. The simulation study results suggested that inaccuracy in estimating the MixSLDA model due to short answers may be overcome by collecting large numbers of answer documents.

Since MixSLDA was proposed in a mixture modelling framework, it shares challenges of mixture modelling (Gelman et al., 2013). For example, the latent classes that are detected partly depend on the functions used for describing the relationship between topic proportions and scores. Also, if latent classes are not sufficiently separated, it may be challenging to detect them well. Given this caveat, the MixSLDA model can be useful for understanding and summarizing the relationships between examinees' writing and scores in that it provides a flexible approach that helps to account for heterogeneity in test performance.

As mentioned earlier, determining the number of topics and the number of latent classes of Mix-SLDA is challenging. In this study, we employed various tools, including Bayesian model selection criteria such as WAIC and LOOCV, to narrow down candidates for the two components. The solution based on these two criteria was further justified by evaluating topic coherence and exclusivity with a fixed number of latent classes and posterior predictive null checks with a fixed number of topics. The interaction between the two components on the use of model selection criteria, however, is not certain and needs further investigation.

Given the challenges in determining appropriate numbers of topics and latent classes, it might be worth comparing our current approach of joint modelling with a two-stage procedure, which fits LDA first and then fits a latent mixture model as this approach does not involve a simultaneous decision regarding the number of topics and the number of latent classes. However, joint modelling is recommended because it is generally known to be more efficient and less biased in the estimates than multistage procedures (e.g., Ibrahim et al., 2010;Sayers et al., 2017;Wu, 2002).

In this paper, we considered the scores as a continuous variable. The MixSLDA can be extended to allow scores in various forms, such as categorical scores, by applying the generalized linear models. In addition, it was assumed in this paper that different latent groups share the same topic structure. Recently, Sridhar et al. (2022) introduced a heterogeneous supervised topic model (HSTM) that allows response-specific topic structure. While HSTM allows heterogeneity in the relationship between individual words and associated responses (i.e., response-specific topic structure), MixSLDA allows to detect distinct but unobserved subpopulations (i.e., latent groups) that have heterogeneous relationships between the context of a document (i.e., topic proportions) and associated responses. While the current MixSLDA assumes that the topic structures are the same across latent groups, future research might consider allowing topics to vary across groups in order to investigate between-group differences in topic structure.

In this study, we employed various tools, including Bayesian model selection criteria such as WAIC and LOOCV, to narrow down candidates for the two components. The solution based on these two criteria was further justified by evaluating topic coherence and exclusivity with a fixed number of latent classes and posterior predictive null checks with a fixed number of topics. The interaction between the two components on the use of model selection criteria, however, is not certain and needs further investigation.

Given the challenges in determining appropriate numbers of topics and latent classes, it might be worth comparing our current approach of joint modelling with a two-stage procedure, which fits LDA first and then fits a latent mixture model as this approach does not involve a simultaneous decision regarding the number of topics and the number of latent classes. However, joint modelling is recommended because it is generally known to be more efficient and less biased in the estimates than multistage procedures (e.g., Ibrahim et al., 2010;Sayers et al., 2017;Wu, 2002).

In this paper, we considered the scores as a continuous variable. The MixSLDA can be extended to allow scores in various forms, such as categorical scores, by applying the generalized linear models. In addition, it was assumed in this paper that different latent groups share the same topic structure. Recently, Sridhar et al. (2022) introduced a heterogeneous supervised topic model (HSTM) that allows response-specific topic structure. While HSTM allows heterogeneity in the relationship between individual words and associated responses (i.e., response-specific topic structure), MixSLDA allows to detect distinct but unobserved subpopulations (i.e., latent groups) that have heterogeneous relationships between the context of a document (i.e., topic proportions) and associated responses. While the current MixSLDA assumes that the topic structures are the same across latent groups, future research might consider allowing topics to vary across groups in order to investigate between-group differences in topic structure.

SLDA model (MixSLDA) for analysing text data and associated scores from CR items. As with other topic models, topics identified from this model can be useful in understanding latent themes in written responses, and the relative use of topics can be useful in investigating the variability in examinees' written responses. This model was designed to detect meaningful heterogeneous latent subgroups with respect to the relationships between topic proportions in text and paired scores. In this model, scores were regressed on topic proportions, and the regression coefficients were allowed to differ across different latent classes. This model was illustrated by analysing an assessment data, and it showed that the MixSLDA could be a useful tool for gaining insights into what and how examinees write. The MixSLDA detected latent topics that summarized examinees' written responses and the relationships between the written responses and associated scores in a straightforward way. Four topics were detected in examinees' written responses, and two latent classes were detected in the relationships between examinees' responses and their scores. Examinees in Class 2 had a higher average score, with a larger proportion of male and eighth grade examinees than those assigned to Class 1. Answer documents of examinees in Class 2 showed to have higher proportions of Topics 2 (i.e., Hypothesis, Observation, and Evidence) and 3 (i.e., Controlling Variables), and lower proportions on Topic 4 (i.e., Everyday Language) compared to those of examinees in Class 1. Furthermore, the results showed that the relationship between scores and Topic 3 differed for the two latent classes. We compared documents with relatively higher Topic 3 proportions (i.e., documents with Topic 3 proportion estimates higher than .45) and focused on how examinees constructed answers for the Topic 3 related questions. Most of the documents from the examinees in Class 2 indicated appropriate responses regarding controlling variables. On the other hand, documents from Class 1 appeared to lack sufficient understanding about independent and dependent variables, as reflected in their use of wrong information in the passages.

We also demonstrated that MixSLDA provided more complete capture of the data than SLDA, which is identical to MixSLDA with one latent class. An SLDA analysis was conducted for the purpose of comparison. Topics from both SLDA and MixSLDA were quite similar, which was not surprising since the two models learned topics from the same text. However, our model comparison indices and posterior predictive checks indicated that SLDA was not sufficient to represent the observed data.

In this study, we observed that some words appeared among the top 20 highest probability words in more than one topic. This is because each word has a probability of occurring in a document for each topic, and some words had high probabilities for more than one topic. Although exclusivity is sometimes a desirable characteristic, mixed membership models such as the SLDA and MixSLDA both allow for this to occur. One way to have more unique topics is to increase the number of topics as indicated in Figure 2, thereby increasing exclusivity. However, in this case, this also can lead to decreasing topic coherence. Topic coherence is often higher for a smaller number of topics because the topics tend to be dominated by common words. On the other hand, as the number of topics increases, topics tend to become specific, resulting in higher exclusivity. The challenge lies in selecting a model that has high values on both topic coherence and exclusivity. In this study, when the number of topics was less than four, topics tended to be more abstract, and when the number of topics was greater than four, some topics tended to be more similar. This model was further investigated by conducting a simulation study under different conditions. Data were generated based on the empirical example data. Results suggested that parameter recovery was influenced by both document size and document length. That is, the mean relative bias and MSE tended to decrease as the number of documents or the average number of words in a document increased. It indicates that having either a large number of documents or documents with sufficient length of words is necessary to obtain accurate parameter estimates. Some assessments have CR items that do not require a long response. The simulation study results suggested that inaccuracy in estimating the MixSLDA model due to short answers may be overcome by collecting large numbers of answer documents.

Since MixSLDA was proposed in a mixture modelling framework, it shares challenges of mixture modelling (Gelman et al., 2013). For example, the latent classes that are detected partly depend on the functions used for describing the relationship between topic proportions and scores. Also, if latent classes are not sufficiently separated, it may be challenging to detect them well. Given this caveat, the MixSLDA model can be useful for understanding and summarizing the relationships between examinees' writing and scores in that it provides a flexible approach that helps to account for heterogeneity in test performance.
